messages.author: Tinker'r K6EH
messages.text: I did testing with a duplicate Station G2 build without the bad firmware (but a much lesser gain antenna, the A915) here a couple weeks ago on a mountain top which is one of the most populated and hottest RF sites in the area. The Station G2 was hearing a RAK repeater some 24 miles away at a -8.50dB SNR. It was not intermittent, and not fluctuating. The 24 mile away repeater hear my Station G2 at a 2.75dB. I tested without filters and the signals fluctuate up and down, but with the filter it is solid.

messages.author: Uncle Lit
messages.text: @Tinker'r K6EH @Ferret @daddycto just FYI, my Station G2 repeater also had the deaf problem. it was very obvious to see with analyzer.letsme.sh/packets. and the fix for me was to use this command: set agc.reset.interval 4 this "resets" the radio every 4 seconds. looking at the code, it is actually just an IDLE command and that just tickles the radio and it changes AGC back to normal. it was pretty obvious to me that it fixed my problem through viewing packets getting reported on analyzer.letsme.sh/packets (comparing Redmond Powerline Trail and Cougar, my repeater should be able to hear 100% of what cougar hears and repeats). Give this a try. it may help.
messages.content_html: @Tinker'r K6EH @Ferret @daddycto just FYI, my Station G2 repeater also had the deaf problem. it was very obvious to see with analyzer.letsme.sh/packets. and the fix for me was to use this command: set agc.reset.interval 4 this "resets" the radio every 4 seconds. looking at the code, it is actually just an IDLE command and that just tickles the radio and it changes AGC back to normal. it was pretty obvious to me that it fixed my problem through viewing packets getting reported on analyzer.letsme.sh/packets (comparing Redmond Powerline Trail and Cougar, my repeater should be able to hear 100% of what cougar hears and repeats). Give this a try. it may help.

messages.author: Ferret
messages.text: think it will do the same for for an e22 or sx chips?
messages.content_html: think it will do the same for for an e22 or sx chips?

messages.author: Uncle Lit
messages.text: I think so. the trigger of the AGC problem usually is because there is very loud out of band signals that the radio hears. when the radio hears it, it turns down AGC. so when a normal level of in-band signal comes in, the AGC is too low for the radio to hear it. radios with on-board LNA like Station G2 and EBYTE E22 maybe more prone to have this deaf problem. (edited)

messages.author: Pigwich
messages.text: Colinear antennas - Got a Comet GP9 - For Not for ISM, but specs are this: 146MHz 8.5dBi 5⁄8 wave x 3 446MHz 11.9dBi 5⁄8 wave x 8 Think of a simple dipole antenna having a donut radiation pattern, sending plenty of power into the sky above and the ground, a colinear has higher gain because the donut is flatter - meaning most power is radiated outwards towards the horizon, not towards the ground or outer space. The more of those 5/8 wave antennas you stack up, the higher the gain. Antenna is still putting out 1 watt, but it's all going out mostly sideways, so everything on the horizon sees better reception and sensitivity, but with a regular 3 db dipole it's more or less everywhere (except for exactly straight up and down)

messages.author: Ferret
messages.text: It seems based on the g2 wiki, the 20w figure is based on a plugged in G2 with no batteries. In which case it would make sense since those power supplies would have a slow response to sudden voltage drops generated by a TX. A larger supply would keep up a little better. However I don't think that's required when power by batteries since they would easily keep up with draws. on a similiar note, the wehooper boards require a battery for this reason and have a hard time running on AC adapters, even 3A ones.

messages.author: white_lightning
messages.text: Activity on the ham repeater is going to be unpredictable. It could be quiet for long periods and then be constant for 30 minutes straight. If it happens to coincide with activity on the MeshCore node, the node receiver could get blanked. This will only happen if the two devices are in relatively close proximity to each other. That's why comm sites can be a problem. There are also still a number of high power paging transmitters as low as 929 which, if near a comm site, will cause problems even with the narrowest filters that we've been discussing. This is why getting away from the band edges is our best bet, IF we can find a relatively quiet frequency. I think the one that was discussed the other night (around 922.3 ?) might be a reasonable place to look.

messages.author: nullrouten
messages.text: Draft email…. (Looking for feedback so I can reach out): ———- Dear Mr. Jung, I’m writing regarding NextNav’s FCC Experimental License WP2XYS for operations in the 902–907 MHz and 918–928 MHz bands in metropolitan San Jose, CA. I operate and help coordinate a community of LoRa / Meshtastic / MeshCore networks in California that relies heavily on the 902–928 MHz ISM band for long-range, low-power links (typically around 100–200 mW, with 10-100 kilometer paths). Because your experimental operations can run at up to 60 dBm ERP from multiple fixed sites in the 918–928 MHz range, we’re concerned about potential in-band interference to these unlicensed systems. Your authorization’s special conditions mention: • A requirement to notify nearby licensees and the unlicensed device user community about test dates, frequencies, and locations, and • The designation of a “stop buzzer” contact for any interference concerns (yourself). To help us plan and to collaborate constructively, could you please share: 1. Planned test schedule • Estimated start and end dates • Daily operating windows (local time) • Whether tests will be continuous or in short campaigns 2. Technical parameters • Exact center frequencies / channelization within 918–928 MHz (and any use of 902–907 MHz for mobiles) • Expected bandwidths and waveforms • Antenna locations and approximate ERP per site 3. Preferred process for interference reports • The best real-time “stop buzzer” contact path (phone/email) • Any specific data you’d like us to capture (timestamps, frequency, RSSI, SNR, screenshots, logs, etc.) to help you diagnose issues quickly We’d like to be good neighbors in the band and, ideally test to minimize conflicts or misconceptions while your experimentation is underway. Having advance notice and some basic technical detail would let us coordinate and provide you with high-quality field observations if any issues arise. Thank you in advance for your time and for any information you can provide. I’m happy to jump on a quick call if that’s easier. Best regards,

messages.author: Ferret
messages.text: Hello Everyone! Based on feedback from last Sunday’s SF test, we’re changing the procedure. The Biggest Change : Moving from a single session to a multi-day trial so more nodes+repeaters can come online and we can properly test settings across the whole network. Test Window → SF7 Profile FQ:927.875 BW:62.5 SF:7 CR:5 → Start: THIS FRIDAY, Dec 12 @ 8pm → End: THIS MONDAY, Dec 15 @ 8pm Repeater Operators → Update the furthest / downstream repeaters first → Then update the closer links in your chain Migration Day → MONDAY, Dec 15 – 8pm → This will become the new SoCal Default THE ASK Be Online FRIDAY, Dec 12 – 8pm Update all your nodes to SF7 ## Help confirm links + coverage
messages.content_html:
Hello Everyone!

Based on feedback from last Sunday’s SF test, we’re changing the procedure.

The Biggest Change: Moving from a single session to a multi-day trial so more nodes+repeaters can come online and we can properly test settings across the whole network.

Test Window → SF7 Profile

FQ:927.875 BW:62.5 SF:7 CR:5 → Start: THIS FRIDAY, Dec 12 @ 8pm → End: THIS MONDAY, Dec 15 @ 8pm

Repeater Operators

→ Update the furthest / downstream repeaters first → Then update the closer links in your chain

Migration Day

→ MONDAY, Dec 15 – 8pm → This will become the new SoCal Default

THE ASK

Be Online FRIDAY, Dec 12 – 8pm

Update all your nodes to SF7

## Help confirm links + coverage

messages.author: nullrouten
messages.text: Have at it ! Private is where you share the exact name and key with friends. Hash is where multiple people just add the same #text and then key is derived in the background , predictably, and all participants are just joined up (less secure, but pretty easy). All channels can be shared with QR code if people don’t like to type
